intro: The biggest real split between Discourse and Front is lock-in posture: Discourse carries low lock-in (oss forum, self-hostable), while Front carries medium lock-in (shared inbox, emails exportable). That single fact shapes the rest of the comparison. Discourse is built for open-source projects and brands wanting indexable, long-form community discussions and is not a fit for teams needing real-time chat or a zero-ops managed experience. Front is built for ops, success, and sales teams handling high-volume email collaboratively and is not a fit for classic helpdesk workflows better served by zendesk or intercom. On the scoring side, Discourse rates 5/5 for developer experience and 5/5 for transparency; Front rates 4/5 and 4/5 respectively. The honest trade-off: with Discourse you accept that self-hosting ruby stack is operational work, while Front comes with the reality that pricing escalates quickly with seats. Neither side is free. Discourse's headline strength in the data is that open-source with mature plugin ecosystem.
quick_take: Discourse is for open-source projects and brands wanting indexable, long-form community discussions; Front is for ops, success, and sales teams handling high-volume email collaboratively; decide based on lock-in tolerance.
verdict_a: Choose Discourse if you are open-source projects and brands wanting indexable, long-form community discussions and can accept low lock-in (oss forum, self-hostable).
verdict_b: Choose Front if you are ops, success, and sales teams handling high-volume email collaboratively and need medium lock-in (shared inbox, emails exportable).

faq.a: Discourse uses a freemium model, while Front uses a seat model; Discourse has a free tier and Front does not. We don't have per-seat numbers in this dataset, so 'cheaper' depends on volume. Price both on your actual usage before deciding.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Discourse to Front?
faq.a: Discourse has low lock-in (oss forum, self-hostable); Front has medium lock-in (shared inbox, emails exportable). Migration effort scales with how much state and automation you've built in Discourse; plan for data export, config rebuild, and a parallel-run period before cutting over.
In our data, Discourse scores 5/5 for developer experience versus 4/5 for Front. That said, DX is subjective; the Front score may still be acceptable if its feature fit is stronger for your use case.

faq.q: Is Front a good alternative to Discourse?
faq.a: Front is built for ops, success, and sales teams handling high-volume email collaboratively and explicitly not for classic helpdesk workflows better served by zendesk or intercom, so it's a fit only if your workflow matches its stated audience. If you were using Discourse for open-source projects and brands wanting indexable, long-form community discussions, the switch is reasonable; outside that scope, look elsewhere.
